I really try and take out the node.

That's the SUMP, S-U-M-P, SUMP lymph node.
Sometimes you have to dig pretty deep to get it.
But what this will do is it'll expose the major fissure from the backside.
And it gives you a landing zone as you're going to try and open up the posterior aspect of the major fissure.
It's a key element in any right upper lobectomy and even a right lower lobectomy.
So you can see how close I am and I'm trying to dissect out that node.

I'm using that sponge roll to sort of sweep and soak up the blood.

on the other side of that node, on the deep side of that node.
And one is the superior segmental pulmonary artery, which of course you don't wanna injure.
And then there's gonna be a posterior segmental pulmonary vein that'll be draining the posterior segment of the right upper lobe.
And I try and get this node out, but I'm always cognizant of the fact that I don't wanna be too deep because you can injure those structures.
